verses.text: Os homens de Zife já tinham se oferecido para capturar Davi e entregá-lo a Saul ( **23.19-20** ). • Haquila... Jesimom é o mesmo local geográfico que haviam mencionado a Saul antes ( **23.19** ). Davi não tinha se afastado muito, provavelmente porque o terreno montanhoso estava repleto de inúmeras cavernas, oferecendo muitos bons esconderijos.

Esta foi a última vez que Saul e Davi estiveram juntos. Os zifeus informaram a Saul pela segunda vez onde Davi estava escondido (veja **23.19-20** ), e Davi poupou a vida de Saul pela segunda vez, assim como fez na caverna de En-Gedi (veja capítulo **24** ).\n\n
Saul havia perseguido Davi novamente, apesar de seu remorso anterior ( **24.17-21** ). Saul se tornara incapaz de agir racionalmente ou falar a verdade.

26:4 David . . . sent out spies . . . and David arose, and came to the place where Saul had pitched—Having obtained certain information of the locality, he seems, accompanied by his nephew (1 Sam 26:6), to have hid himself, perhaps disguised, in a neighboring wood, or hill, on the skirts of the royal camp towards night, and waited to approach it under covert of the darkness.
Abner, filho de Ner: Veja nota de estudo em 20.25 .

Esta é a única referência a Aimeleque, o heteu. • Zeruia era irmã de Davi ( **1Cr 2.16** ), então Abisai era sobrinho de Davi. Os filhos de Zeruia (Abisai, Joabe e Asael) foram três dos seguidores mais fiéis de Davi (e.g., **2Sm 2.18** ), mas às vezes causavam dificuldades a Davi (veja **2Sm 3.39** ; **16.10** ; **19.22** ).

26:7 So David and Abishai came to the people by night,.... Josephus (l) wrongly says, that he took with him both Abishai and Ahimelech; he chose to have but one, for the greater secrecy. This was a bold enterprise, for two men to go into a camp of three thousand men, though it was in the night; when though they might suppose the greater part of them were asleep, yet they could not well suppose this of all, and especially of the guards or sentinels;
Deus certamente entregou seu inimigo a você: Cp. com **24.4** .
o ungido do Senhor: Veja nota de estudo em 24.6 .
Davi estava confiante de que Deus julgaria entre ele e Saul, assim como havia feito entre ele e Nabal (veja **25.38** ). Saul morreu... em batalha contra os filisteus ( **31.6** ).

26:11 the spear that is at his bolster, and the cruse of water—The Oriental spear had, and still has, a spike at the lower extremity, intended for the purpose of sticking the spear into the ground when the warrior is at rest. This common custom of Arab sheiks was also the practice of the Hebrew chiefs. at his bolster—literally, "at his head"; perhaps, Saul as a sovereign had the distinguished luxury of a bolster carried for him. A "cruse of water" is usually, in warm climates, kept near a person's couch, as a drink in the night time is found very refreshing.

26:12 David took the spear and the cruse - The spear, we have already seen, was the emblem of power and regal dignity. But it is usual, in Arab camps, for every man to have his lance stuck in the ground beside him, that he may be ready for action in a moment. The cruse of water resembled, in some measure, the canteens of our soldiers. In such a climate, where water was always scarce, it was necessary for each man to carry a little with him, to refresh him on his march.

26:13 Then David . . . stood on the top of an hill afar off . . . and cried to the people—(See on Jdg 9:7). The extraordinary purity and elasticity of the air in Palestine enable words to be distinctly heard that are addressed by a speaker from the top of one hill to people on that of another, from which it is separated by a deep intervening ravine. Hostile parties can thus speak to each other, while completely beyond the reach of each other's attack.
Abner: Veja a nota de estudo em 20.25 .

26:15 David said to Abner, Art not thou a valiant man: . . . wherefore then hast thou not kept thy lord the king?—The circumstance of David having penetrated to the center of the encampment, through the circular rows of the sleeping soldiers, constituted the point of this sarcastic taunt. This new evidence of David's moderation and magnanimous forbearance, together with his earnest and kindly expostulation, softened the obduracy of Saul's heart.
merecem morrer: A morte era a punição para a negligência no dever.
É você, meu filho Davi? Cp. **24.16** .

26:18 And he said, wherefore doth my lord thus pursue after his servant?.... Suggesting that it was both below him to do it, and against his interest; for David was his servant, and he would gladly have continued in his service, and done his business, but he drove him from it, and pursued him as a traitor, when he had not been guilty of any offence to his knowledge: and therefore puts the following questions: for what have I done? or what evil is in mine hand?

Se o Senhor incitou você contra mim: Davi estava perplexo com o comportamento de Saul e preocupado com a possibilidade de ter cometido um pecado não intencional. • um esquema humano: Possivelmente uma referência a Doegue ( **22.9-10** ). • adorar deuses pagãos: O sentimento é semelhante a **Sl 137.4** . Qualquer pessoa exilada da terra santa não podia adorar o Senhor participando do sacrifício no santuário central.
uma única pulga: Cp. **24.14** .

Eu pequei: Saul já havia reconhecido o erro de seus caminhos uma vez antes ( **24.16-19** ). Davi não tinha um bom motivo para confiar nele desta vez (veja **27.1** ). • Eu fui um tolo: Este termo hebraico ( sakal, “agir como tolo,” veja também **13.13** ) é diferente da palavra que significa “tolo” (Nabal) usada no capítulo **25** . No entanto, as duas palavras têm significados paralelos. Saul e Nabal são figuras paralelas.

26:22 And David answered and said, behold the king's spear!.... And which perhaps was his sceptre, and which David therefore would not keep, lest it should be thought or said that he had deprived him of an ensign of his royalty, and be interpreted as a token of his design to seize his crown and throne: and let one of the young men come over and fetch it; for notwithstanding the acknowledgment Saul had made of his sin and folly, David did not choose to carry the spear to him;
Davi não prejudicou Saul ( **24.6** ; **26.9** ) porque Saul era o ungido do Senhor (veja nota de estudo em 24.6 ).
Que o Senhor valorize minha vida, assim como valorizei a sua: Davi não podia contar com Saul para retribuir. Assim, seu destino estava nas mãos de Deus.
Saul voltou para casa, e esse pode ter sido o último encontro pessoal de Davi com ele.
